planting.note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days before sowing to improve germination; may bloom the second year.

directions: Cold-stratify the seed in moist sand for about a month, then sow shallowly in spring once soil has warmed. This native prairie perennial sends down a deep taproot, so choose a permanent, well-drained location in full sun. Drooping lavender-pink petals surround the spiky central cone and draw bees and butterflies through summer. Established plants tolerate drought and poor soil; leave seed heads standing to feed goldfinches in fall.

planting.note: Surface-sow and press in; seed needs light to germinate.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the soil surface without covering it, since light aids germination. This reddish-pink heirloom yarrow forms feathery, aromatic foliage topped with flat clusters of bloom above soft leaves. Once established it is exceptionally drought-tolerant and thrives in lean, well-drained soil in full sun. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong bloom, and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Surface-sow; needs light to germinate. Self-seeds readily.

directions: Scatter seed on prepared soil and barely press it in, as light triggers germination. This easy native annual, also called calliopsis or golden tickseed, opens bright yellow petals marked with burgundy centers from early summer to fall. It thrives in poor, dry soil and full sun, quickly filling meadows and borders with color. Allow some flowers to set seed and it will reliably return the following season.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes.

directions: Sow seed directly after the last frost, one-half inch deep in warm, well-drained soil. This branching native annual produces many small yellow flowers with reddish-brown centers on graceful stems. It is highly drought-tolerant and thrives on the lean soils of open meadows and restoration plantings. Bees feed on the blooms while finches and other birds harvest the ripe seed in fall.

planting.note: Cold-moist stratify for 30 days to improve germination; often blooms the second year from seed.

directions: Give seed a month of cold-moist stratification, then sow in spring for the strongest stands. This hardy native perennial bears purple petals around a raised, coppery cone from summer into fall. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil where it will tolerate heat and drought once established. Leave the seed heads standing over winter to feed songbirds and encourage self-sowing.

planting.note: Scarify and cold-stratify seed; inoculating with legume rhizobia aids nitrogen fixation.

directions: Lightly scarify and cold-stratify the seed, then sow into a firm, weed-free seedbed in full sun. This native legume fixes nitrogen and produces cone-shaped clusters of vivid purple flowers in early to mid-summer. Its deep taproot makes it very drought-tolerant and slow to establish, so be patient in the first year. It is a superb choice for prairie restoration, pollinator plantings, and improving lean soils.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors; seed needs light and warmth to germinate. Transplant after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors on the soil surface under bright light and warmth, as it needs light to sprout. Grown for its painted foliage rather than flowers, this mix shows a wide range of leaf colors and patterns. Move plants outdoors only after all frost danger passes, choosing a spot with partial shade and consistent moisture. Pinch growing tips regularly and remove flower spikes to keep foliage bushy and vivid; it can be overwintered indoors as a houseplant.

planting.note: Surface-sow on prepared soil in early spring or fall; needs light and does not transplant well.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the soil surface in early spring or fall, as it needs light and resents transplanting. This iconic Flanders poppy opens tissue-paper scarlet petals with dark centers in late spring and early summer. Grow it in full sun and well-drained soil where its deep taproot makes it drought-tolerant. Let flowers form seed pods and it will self-sow generously for years to come.

planting.note: Scarify hard seed coat before sowing to speed germination.

directions: Nick or soak the hard seed coat before sowing to encourage even germination in warm soil. This native hibiscus produces large, pink, tropical-looking blooms and thrives in moist to wet ground. Give it full sun and steady water, making it ideal for pond edges, rain gardens, and low, damp spots. The plant dies back in winter and returns from the root, drawing bees and hummingbirds each summer.

planting.note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; often blooms the second year.

directions: Cold-stratify the seed for about a month, then sow into moist soil in spring. Also called swamp milkweed, this native perennial bears clusters of fragrant dusty-rose flowers atop narrow-leaved stems. Unlike common milkweed it tolerates wet ground, making it perfect for rain gardens and pond margins in full sun. It is an essential Monarch host and nectar source, so avoid pesticides and let caterpillars feed on the foliage.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; succession sow for continuous cut flowers.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep directly in the garden after the last frost once soil has warmed. This heirloom variety produces deep burgundy-red, velvety petals around dark chocolate centers on sturdy five- to six-foot stems. Give it full sun and moderate water, staking in windy sites to protect the tall stalks. Cut blooms just as they open for long-lasting arrangements, or leave heads to feed pollinators and birds.

planting.note: Surface-sow; seed needs light to germinate.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the soil surface without covering, since it needs light to sprout. This heirloom sweet alyssum forms a low, spreading carpet of honey-scented violet-purple blooms. Grow it in full sun to light shade as an edging, container filler, or ground cover among vegetables to draw beneficial insects. Shear lightly after the first flush to encourage a fresh round of bloom into cooler weather.

planting.note: Scarify and soak seed before sowing; resents root disturbance so direct sow when possible.

directions: Nick and soak the hard-coated seed overnight, then sow where plants are to grow since lupines dislike transplanting. This perennial hybrid sends up tall spikes densely packed with pea-like flowers in blue, purple, pink, yellow, and white. Plant in full sun and cool, well-drained soil; as a legume it fixes nitrogen and improves the ground. Cut spent spikes to encourage rebloom and let a few set seed for next season's plants.

planting.note: Surface-sow and press in; needs light to germinate. Usually blooms the second year.

directions: Press seed onto the soil surface without burying it, since light aids germination. This classic perennial produces crisp white petals around bright yellow centers on strong stems, and the flowers are edible. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, deadheading regularly to extend the summer bloom. Divide crowded clumps every two to three years in spring to keep plants healthy and floriferous.

planting.note: Surface-sow; needs light to germinate. Spreads vigorously by runners.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and press it in lightly, as light encourages germination. Commonly called pink ladies, this spreading native perennial opens delicate pink, cup-shaped flowers over a long season. It thrives on neglect in full sun and dry, poor soil, spreading readily by runners to cover slopes and difficult sites. Give it room to roam or contain it, and expect a carefree groundcover loved by bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; often blooms the second year.

directions: Cold-stratify the seed for about a month before sowing in spring for reliable germination. This native perennial produces large, fragrant clusters of soft pink star-shaped flowers on upright stems with broad, fuzzy leaves. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil where it tolerates drought and spreads gradually by rhizomes. It is a vital host for Monarch and Queen butterfly caterpillars, so keep it pesticide-free.

planting.note: Sow shallowly and cover; seed germinates best in darkness.

directions: Sow seed lightly covered, since this annual phlox germinates best in darkness. It produces a colorful blend of five-petaled, lightly fragrant blooms on low, spreading plants. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, sowing in early spring or fall in mild climates for the best bloom. Deadhead to prolong flowering and enjoy the bright carpets it forms in beds, borders, and containers.

planting.note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; often blooms the second year.

directions: Give seed a month of cold-moist stratification, then sow in spring. This dependable native perennial bears clouds of small lavender-blue, daisy-like flowers in late summer and fall. Plant in full sun and average, well-drained soil where its smooth blue-green foliage resists disease. It provides critical late-season nectar for bees and migrating butterflies as other flowers fade.

planting.note: Surface-sow; light aids germination. Short-lived but self-sows.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and press it in, as light helps it germinate. Also called dotted horsemint, this native produces tiered whorls of yellow flowers spotted with purple, set off by showy pink bracts. It thrives in full sun and sandy, dry soil, and its aromatic foliage deters deer. A biennial to short-lived perennial, it self-sows freely and is a magnet for bees, wasps, and other beneficial pollinators.

planting.note: Cold-moist stratify seed for 30 days; surface-sow as light aids germination.

directions: Cold-stratify the seed for a month and surface-sow, since light improves germination. This tall native perennial produces large, domed clusters of fragrant rose-pink flowers from August into September. Grow it in full sun to part shade and consistently moist to wet soil, ideal for rain gardens and pond edges. Its towering stems are a butterfly favorite and add bold structure to the back of the border.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; good for containers due to compact habit.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep after the last frost once soil warms. This dwarf heirloom grows just two to three feet tall, producing dozens of fluffy, double golden-yellow pom-pom blooms. Its compact, branching habit suits containers, borders, and small gardens, and the sturdy stems make fine cut flowers. Give it full sun and moderate water, and deadhead to keep new blooms coming through the season.

planting.note: Sow indoors and cover; seed germinates best in darkness and cool conditions.

directions: Start seed indoors covered and in darkness, keeping the soil cool for best germination. This heirloom mix produces large, velvety, edible blossoms in blue, purple, yellow, orange, and burgundy. Pansies love cool weather, so transplant in early spring or fall and grow in full sun to part shade with steady moisture. Deadhead often and they will bloom prolifically until summer heat or hard freezes arrive.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors; seed needs light to germinate. Transplant when hardened off.

directions: Start seed indoors on the soil surface under light, since it needs light to germinate. This mix produces tall, upright spikes of ruffled, nectar-rich blooms in vivid colors. Snapdragons prefer cool weather, so transplant in early spring for strong stems and abundant flowers. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and cut spikes regularly for long-lasting bouquets and repeat bloom.

planting.note: Sow in fall; scarify seed and inoculate with rhizobia for best results. Blooms the following spring.

directions: Nick or soak the hard seed coat and sow in fall so plants establish for spring bloom. The state flower of Texas, this native annual lupine forms spikes of striking blue flowers tipped with white. Grow in full sun and dry, well-drained soil where, as a legume, it fixes nitrogen and needs little care. Let plants set seed to naturalize, and expect the strongest displays after a cool, moist winter.

planting.note: Soak seed overnight and sow deep; resents transplanting so direct sow.

directions: Soak the large seed overnight and sow about half an inch deep directly in the garden after frost. This dwarf, non-trailing variety produces a cheerful mix of yellow, red, and orange blooms held above the foliage. Both the peppery leaves and flowers are edible, making it a favorite for salads and companion planting. Grow in full sun and lean soil, since rich ground yields lots of leaves but fewer flowers.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors under warmth and light; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface in warm, bright conditions, then transplant after all frost has passed. Also called the electric daisy, this tender herb bears yellow and red eyeball-shaped blooms on low, spreading plants. Chewing a flower or leaf produces a tingling, numbing sensation prized in specialty cuisine and herbal remedies. Grow it in full sun and moist, rich soil, treating it as an annual outside frost-free zones or overwintering it indoors.

planting.note: Surface-sow; seed needs light to germinate. Often blooms the second year.

directions: Press seed onto the soil surface without covering, since light is needed for germination. Also called Carpathian bellflower, this compact perennial forms neat mounds covered in violet-blue, upward-facing bells. Grow it in full sun to part shade and cool, well-drained soil, ideal for rock gardens, edgings, and containers. Shear after the first bloom flush to tidy the plant and encourage a second round of flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; branching habit produces many blooms.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep directly in the garden after the last frost once soil warms. This heirloom bears velvety, multi-toned blooms in mahogany, bronze, and burnt orange on branching five- to seven-foot stems. Its many pollen-rich flowers make excellent cut material and feed bees throughout summer and fall. Give it full sun and moderate water, staking tall plants in exposed sites to prevent lodging.

planting.note: Surface-sow and press in; seed needs light to germinate.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the soil surface without burying it, as light aids germination. This native heirloom yarrow bears flat clusters of white flowers above soft, feathery, aromatic foliage. Extremely drought-tolerant once established, it thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil. Deadhead to prolong bloom and divide clumps every few years; the flowers are valued in medicinal and dye gardens and dry well for arrangements.

planting.note: Grown indoors year-round on trays; sow densely and harvest as young shoots.

directions: Sow the small seed densely across a moist tray or shallow container of medium, indoors year-round. Keep evenly moist and provide bright, indirect light once the shoots emerge and green up. Harvest the tender, nutty micro shoots with scissors in about a week when the first leaves appear. Rinse and eat fresh in salads and sandwiches; no thinning or spacing is needed for this quick indoor crop.
